figures.caption: FIG. 2. Symmetry-resolved map of the (normalized) two-dimensional spin-flip SANS cross section for a linear vortex ensemble. Each panel shows the analytically calculated spin-flip scattering pattern [up to (qR)max = 5.0] obtained from Eq. (6) for a given combination of the vortex amplitude parameter m1R/m0 (vertical axis) and the cone angle αc (horizontal axis), which describes the statistical distribution of vortex axes within a rotational cone. The model represents an ensemble of linearly varying vortex structures whose axes are distributed within a cone of opening angle αc, rather than a single vortex configuration. The background colors indicate the dominant angular anisotropy of the scattering pattern: four-fold symmetry ∝ 1− cos(4θ) (light blue), vertical two-fold symmetry ∝ 1 − cos(2θ) (dark blue), horizontal two-fold symmetry ∝ 3 + cos(2θ) (orange), isotropic ring-like scattering ∝ 1 (green), and intermediate transition regions. The map provides a controlled visual classification tool for experimental two-dimensional spin-flip SANS data, linking observed angular anisotropies to specific regions in the parameter space of vortex amplitude and axis distribution.

FIG. 3. Same as Fig. 2, but based on the nonlinear hyperbolic vortex magnetization ansatz Eq. (10) and numerically computed using the NuMagSANS software package [22]. In this calculation, we used spherical nanoparticles with a radius of R = 20nm (discretized into a 2 × 2 × 2 nm3 grid) and 1024 samples for the rotation angles α, β. The vortex parameter ν in Eq. (10) is selected as ν = µ = m1R/m0, as the hyperbolic vortex (10) reduces (in a first-order Taylor expansion at ρ′ = 0) to the linear vortex ansatz (1). The resulting two-dimensional scattering cross sections are shown up to a maximum scattering vector of qmax = 0.25 nm−1. The code used to generate the data is available on Zenodo [23].
